title: Dog Ate Ibuprofen: Emergency Triage Guide & Next Steps
meta_desc: If your dog ate ibuprofen, this is a high-priority medical emergency. Learn the risks of toxicity and immediate actions to take for your pet.
content_html:
TL;DR: Ibuprofen is highly toxic to dogs and causes life-threatening damage to the kidneys and stomach; seek emergency veterinary care immediately if your dog ingests any amount, even if they appear normal.

What is ibuprofen toxicity and why is it dangerous for my dog?

Ibuprofen is a common over-the-counter human medication used for pain relief, but it is highly toxic to dogs. Unlike humans, dogs cannot process this drug safely, which can lead to severe damage to the stomach lining and the kidneys. We understand how scary this situation is, but knowing the facts is the first step in helping your pet.

Is ibuprofen ingestion a medical emergency for my dog?

  • Seek veterinary care or contact a pet poison control hotline immediately, as ibuprofen ingestion is a high-priority emergency.
  • Do not wait for your dog to show signs of illness; internal damage can begin long before outward symptoms appear.
  • Take prompt action to provide the best possible chance for a positive outcome.

How does taking a photo of the medication label help the triage process?

  • Take a clear photo of the pill bottle or packaging label if you still have it.
  • Allow the veterinary team to see the exact dosage (milligrams) and any other active ingredients involved.
  • Help the vet quickly calculate the level of toxicity based on your dog's weight, which saves precious minutes during the intake process.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Ibuprofen has a narrow margin of safety in dogs; prolonged use at 5 mg/kg may cause gastric ulcers and perforations. Single acute ingestions of 100-125 mg/kg can cause vomiting, diarrhea, nausea, abdominal pain, and anorexia; renal failure may follow dosages of 175-300 mg/kg; CNS effects can be seen at dosages >400 mg/kg, and dosages >600 mg/kg are potentially lethal. Treatment consists of early decontamination by inducing emesis in recent exposures followed by activated charcoal administration, which can be repeated every 6-8 hours. H1-receptor antagonists may be useful in treating GI ulcers.

Chapter: Toxicology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3028)

faqs.question: What are the common symptoms of ibuprofen poisoning in dogs?
faqs.answer: Symptoms can include vomiting (sometimes with blood), black or tarry stools, abdominal pain, loss of appetite, lethargy, and in severe cases, tremors or seizures.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog a human dose of ibuprofen for their pain?
faqs.answer: No. You should never give a dog ibuprofen. Even a small dose can cause life-threatening gastric ulcers or kidney failure. Always consult your vet for pet-safe pain management.

faqs.question: What should I do if I can't reach my regular vet?
faqs.answer: Contact the nearest 24-hour emergency animal hospital or a pet poison control hotline like the ASPCA Animal Poison Control Center or the Pet Poison Helpline immediately.
